Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
comments.content:
Good Evening,

May the waxing moon bring more of what you want, and not more of what you don't!

To Share: 1. I've been keeping up with weekly posts to my new blog Rhetoric for the Renaissance Man, this week hitting on some thoughts on how to be more "productive," while keeping in mind that at such approaches tend to dehumanize us and lead to overly much emphasis on "efficiency" without tying it into what we actually want. I've tried to counteract that, but I'll let readers be the judge on well I've done: https://rhetoricfortherenaissanceman.com/2025/11/19/getting-things-done-part-1-getting-concrete/

2. This might be veering too close to the Sun of our host's "no AI" rule, but in an Icarian mood, I venture to try to share nonetheless, as I think it might be worthwhile anyhow (no worries if you disagree and don't put this through, JMG). The reason I put it in regardless is that the post I'm about to link compares "model collapse" in AI systems to dysfunctions in non-AI systems (like overly urbanized humans) in a way that I think helps illuminate the coming decline of our situation that will resonate with Ecosophians in general. To be fair, I think the author is mostly, rather than completely, right, and so misses some key insights: https://alwaysthehorizon.substack.com/p/demographic-decline-a-long-term-sociology

To Ask: I've just read Mark Stavish's Egregores, and while it was quite illuminating, it left me with some questions:

1. Do you, JMG (or commentariat), believe that "egregores" are fundamentally flawed/dangerous? Stavish seems to think they're at best neutral, and often harmful.
2. Do you, JMG (or again, the commentariat), believe that egregores can obtain a certain amount of autonomy or "consciousness" even when they are not "possessed" or "ensouled" by a non-corporeal being?
3. Lastly, do you, JMG (or commentariat), believe that egregores are a necessary intermediate step between mortal experience of non-corporeal consciousnesses? In other words, can we only talk to Gods and angels via egregores?

Looking back over those questions, I realize they come across as a bit confrontational, which is not the intent, just trying to be as direct as possible.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content:
I'm not going to delete this post, but please in the future don't put general Ecosophia content in Magic Monday posts. Those belong in the open post on the blog, and you know, that's only a couple of days away.

As for your questions:

1) That's like asking whether human relationships are fundamentally flawed/dangerous. There isn't actually much difference, in fact, because every relationship between people establishes an egregor; you've probably noticed the way that families develop collective personalities distinct from their members, for example.

2) They can have autonomy, but they never develop consciousness.

3) No, but getting past the egregor requires a freedom from cultural relationships that takes a long time and much work to develop. If you set out to interact with a god, let's say, your experience of the god will be shaped by the egregor built up around him by his worshippers. That remains the case until and unless you have reached a level of consciousness far enough above the mass mind that you're free of its influence, and can interact with the god more directly -- a condition generally called "mystical experience."

replies.content:
JMG,
The fact that mystical experience, transcendence and contact with higher hierarchies do not need to be mediated by spirituality was treated academically by Robert Charles Zaehner. He called this “Natural Mystical Experience.”

This leads me to two questions:
1. How can this contact or elevation of the soul be done consciously?
2. Since religions and the various forms of spirituality resonate in the astral, what “Truth” would stand above them?

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content:
1) Various forms of meditation seem to be the most reliable ways there.

2) In the model I use, there are four planes above the astral -- the mental, spiritual, causal, and divine planes. All of them are more complex, intricately textured, populated, and full of meaning than the planes below them, and the intensity of each of these things increases as you go from the mental plane toward the divine plane. Thus it's not a matter of one truth standing above the astral forms of religion. There are whole universes of meaning, value, insight, wisdom, and enlightenment above them, and the further you go, the bigger it gets.

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content: Is it possible to tune in or tune out particular aspects of an egregore by regulating one's engagement with the cultural consciousness? For example, I am drawn Jesus and His Father and their Spirit, but I recognize that the broader Christian egregore contains things I want nothing to do with (e.g., a certain streak of paranoid exclusivism and bigotry, or a prudish suspicion of sex and desire). Can I steer around these obstacles by avoiding groups, spaces, and writings where they're prominent? Conversely, can I gain a truer experience of God by limiting the writings of the mystics and saints who have encountered Him directly?

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content:
I don't think I'm strong enough to resist the pull of a large egregore, so I avoid churches.
Instead, my study buddy and I meet once a week and go over a topic, I pray daily in the morning, read a page or two in my Bible at bedtime, listen to Christian pop music on the radio when I'm driving and think about the religious things I'm working on/with at various times of the day.

It can be argued that the current crop of Christian pop music has its own egregore, or at least it is its own "denomination".

Working on my own Christianity I am finding myself going into realms unspoken-of in any "narrative about Christianity" that I have encountered. And I am confronting some tough topics right now, including having some pretty blunt and forthright things to say to Jesus. He is reacting more like the Jesus portrayed in the current Christian pop music songs and unlike the 'frowning man with a gavel in his hand' that some portray Him as. In short, very patient. I feel free in talking to Him, which is a huge gift.

Perhaps I am swimming through the intersections of several egregores.
Whatever it is, it is lovely freedom to explore and learn what is really going on with this guy who spent some mortal time in Galilee, without guilt, shame, fear, or too many Sunday School preconceptions.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content: Avoidance isn't enough by itself. You have to fill the space with something else. Be aware that many of the mystics and saints had those same attitudes you object to in the mainstream church; you'll need to look for sources that not merely rejected those attitudes but offered good reasons for some other point of view.

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content:
Thanks for including the commentariat as well, one of the things I like the most about the community here is the conversations.

I once described the inevitability of egregors for the mind as the ground are for feet and even though it is flawed, I like the notion of it because it is the basic things we use to move in that liminal space. Many times for example during my explorations I have come across things I don't recall seeing at all but on inspection it is related to the thing I am exploring with my mind and part of something that doesn't seem mine, so my mind is using forms from the collective, associated to that thing, to try to give it a shape. The existentialists described being as being-in-relationship, that is, we don't exist as an isolated self but rather we are a set of selfs that are in the world in different ways. Being the limited beings we are, I like to think that we don't have a framework by ourselves to make sense of a buzzing world of confusion so we depend on the forms we inherit, through stories and the senses or through the larger web, and the forms which we pick up when we relate to a particular subject to give it meaning.

I think that is why sometimes when you push a little further, for example in a magically charged space for meditation, one can have the sensation of falling, speeding, dizziness or in extreme cases of the fright of having no reference for the mind to hang on to. I think that experience might be related to not really having a form either personal or collective to give a shape to an experience we can't describe, so it feels like a void. At least that's how I've experienced it, but then again, I am a little weird and might just be my way of relating to some experiences.

I saw this quote from Lovecraft today that I think a little relevant in regards to depending on egregors.

The most merciful thing in the world, I think, is the inability of the human mind to correlate all its contents. We live on a placid island of ignorance in the midst of black seas of infinity, and it was not meant that we should voyage far. The sciences, each straining in its own direction, have hitherto harmed us little; but some day the piecing together of dissociated knowledge will open up such terrifying vistas of reality, and of our frightful position therein, that we shall either go mad from the revelation or flee from the light into the peace and safety of a new dark age.

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content: I adore that Lovecraft quote. It sums up most of my attitude toward modern science.

replies.title: Re: Getting Things Done, Model Collapse, and Questions on Egregores
replies.content:
It's a great quote! It made me want to read the whole story. I just found my copy of some collected works from Weird Tales that I got at the Lovecraft Museum during one of the potlucks. Unpacking is hard! Both physically and metaphysically in meditation, and both reveal terrifying vistas of reality. Fortunately there are merciful beings that help in both cases. Ah, gratitude. Such a wonderful thing! Much of the fear of the unknown goes away when there are people willing to help and show you the way.

I wonder if modern science has reached a point where the fear of the infinite makes it easier to squirrel away into neat little mental squares than to accept the help of the gods in leading the way.

replies.title: Druid theurgy

replies.title: Re: Druid theurgy
replies.content: We have the testimony of the great Pagan Neoplatonists, among others, to show that prayer need not be limited to the Absolute. For that matter, many devout Christians have directed their prayers to saints and angels as well as the Trinity directly. Human beings occupy a very modest place in the great scheme of things, and we can benefit from the help of other beings higher on the scale than we are. After all, as I pointed out in my book A World Full of Gods, is it any really less arrogant to say "I will only worship the greatest possible god" than to say "I will only drive the most expensive possible car"?

comments.title: Raphael Archangel
comments.content:
Today I would like to express my gratitude to Raphael Archangel and the Malakim, the host of angels working with him, for helping me speedily in the process of healing something that has hurt for too long. May your light shine far and bright to bring much healing and wisdom to the ones seeking it.

I suffered from heavy bullying when I was a teenager, and I took refuge on my wits to help my ego not totally collapse under pressure. That stuck and soon enough what once helped me to survive got corrupted and turned into a weapon that I used to belittle the world, and bitterly mock what I would consider stupid, slowly blocking me from enjoying life and others. Raphael, among many others things, is the archangel that helps to turn the vice of pride into virtue, and after many failed attempts by my own hand I have started to see how my intellectual pride has cast a shadow in how I see the world. With their help I have started to learn to relate to the world in more helpful ways and to use that intelligence to build for myself and others, not to destroy. So thank you Raphael and your Malakim for guiding me, and also clearly letting me know that healing not only happens by divine intervention and asking for it, no matter how many tears, but rather by letting myself open up for the light to come through better and doing the work myself with that. And also thank you JMG for letting us use this space to thank our deities and learn more about ourselves.

I am happy to hear that!

I can recommend some things, but since I don't know your background I will be general.

First of all, pray. These days prayer is misunderstood because prayer is not just talking or just asking for things, but rather it is an attitude of openness. Offerings help a lot in this regard, not because they need offerings, but because by offering something you open up, you can even offer your time and self, in the same way you offer your time and self for volunteering somewhere without pay. If you can't relate to the feeling in order to bring it up at will, remember how it feels to help someone selflessly, when you are open to listening to someone you admire and respect, that is the openness. You can also offer a candle and light it up with that intent. Approach prayer that way and things happen and then just talk to him, tell him what hurts or just let him know you need help. Tell him if he is willing to send one of his angels, the Malakim, in order to help you out, as they are the messengers that help us in regeneration and attain balance, and keep it. But not keeping it in a conclusive or static manner but by facilitating the points of balance where harmony can be kept in motion. They facilitate self-knowledge and help us find our purpose and the courage to pursue it. Then spend at least 2-5 minutes listening in receptive silence. Not waiting, but listening. Pay attention to your mind, your feelings or your surroundings for an answer. In particular pay attention to the screen of your mind. See, one big misconception about beings without physical bodies these days is that they appear to you in one burst of a mystical experience, or like a ghost in front of you and speak. That sometimes happens, but it is not the norm, gods usually communicate through the world: by something happening at just the right moment, perhaps when you are thinking about them, perhaps out of the blue, perhaps when something catches your eye or by the words somebody else says that makes you go "what did you just say?", and they might've not been connected with what he was saying but it made sense to you. Just like that.

If you have a meditation practice here are some thoughts to explore. Raphael is the archangel of the Sun and corresponds to Tiphareth in the Tree of Life. He represents balance and harmony and in Tiphareth resides the Prince of Peace and the Christ within each of us. One concept about it is sacrifice for regeneration and new life.

In Hebrew Raphael is spelled רפאל and it means The Healing of God. Rapha means doctor, or surgeon and El references, Elohim, God thus, healed by god, the healing of god, god's medicine. You can meditate also on each of the Hebrew letters, or scry them if you know how.

Resh, ר, is the head, beginnings, the Sun and it's energy that gives life and conquers all, love and hate.

Peh, פ, represents Mars, change, transformation and the dissolution of what no longer is helpful. As well as the proper use of language and sound for healing. Where Resh and the sun emanante, Peh distributes, if the heart pumps and sends vitality through the blood, just like the sun and its rays, its the iron in it that delivers its Word everywhere. Peh in hebrew also means mouth, as the instrument of speech, but also as the instrument if ingestion. What we consume, in food, thought and deed and as such also what we communicate as well in words and actions. It's the instrument through which our self resonates and carries our will to the world.

Aleph, א, the power of Life, it represents air as well the breath, another reference that air carries.

Lamed, ל, represents the Law. It has to do with the corrective action taken when one has strayed out of balance to bring it back into harmony, or said another way, back in tune with Creation.

His symbols are birds, singing birds in particular, squirrels, the east, sunrise, yellow flowers and butterflies as well as monkeys. So pay attention to those in waking life as well as in dreams. The virtue Raphael helps us attain is devotion and aspiration to the Great Work and recognizing the glory of Life and Creation and the vice it helps of conquer is pride, arrogance and haughtiness: the excess of self-esteem and the feeling that one is above others or the abuse of authority. This cuts you off from receiving, as you can't see what others offer and as such it distances us from feeling connected to others and prevents further growth.

Of course you can also link up all that with magical techniques, but by themselves and reflecting on them they can do a lot.

comments.title: A strong encounter in the grove of air
comments.content:
Hello

Working through the exercises in your book "Celtic Golden Dawn", amidst the pathworkings from water to air the guide told me that Belisama herself is waiting for me in the destination grove, the inner grove of air. Sure enough, when we got that far, the whole grove was alive with a vivid incorporeal presence that, for lack of better words, was there but not "visually" or in any kind of same manner as the guide, for instance, was there.

This presence was awesome, as in awe-inspiring, filling the whole grove with strong energies of a morning in a springtime. There was vigor, playfullness, good cheer, but also visions and high hopes, motivation and the general sense of being awake, to truly see and experience. At the end of it, she promised me to help me out with a difficulty I have been having, and within the same day, the circumstances ran such as forcing me into an uncomfortable situation where the difficulty had to be faced honestly and squarely - and it was resolved in an agreeable manner.

The experience transcended anything I have encountered in the pathworkings before. I was not expecting anything of the sorts. Though now looking back, in the previous pathworking the grove of air was unusually active and responsive, with the Hawk of May appearing in a manner that felt personal and "alive", also offering practical assistance in the mundane life that I gladly accepted.

Now what to make of it all? Is this something that can be expected to happen with this kind of work?

Of course I pursue the work as before. Though even now, thinking back to the experience, I feel a faint whisper of the energies that were there, with a subtle but strong connection to them still. Now I am curious about the next pathworking beginning with the grove of air, but I am not loading high expectations to it. Just curiosity.

I wonder though, being the governing force of the element of air, is it appropriate to offer prayers to Belisama? Or could I, for instance, use the pentagram ritual of air in the summoning form to rouse those energise before contacting her? Or am I better off just sticking with the program for now?

Interacting with such high powers is a new thing to me and I would prefer to be wise about it. Also I am not certain of the difference of an Elemental Lady and say a God or Goddess ruling a sphere. In the bardic grade work the grove of air is the grove of air, but in the tree of life it is also Byth, which is the realm of Mabion, with whom I have had some faint but very different kind of subtle interaction. Further, the element of air has, as far as I recall, always been the strongest in me, so I am wary of creating deeper imbalances.

Also, any insights you might have of this whole thing are very much welcome.

replies.title: Re: A strong encounter in the grove of air
replies.content:
Excellent. Yes, this sometimes happens, and it's becoming more common as more people are invoking the old Celtic gods with the tools of high magic. I recommend sticking with the program for now; once you've finished the Bardic grade you'll have a clearer sense of the whole picture, and if you go on to the Druid grade and complete it, you'll have a set of more potent tools you can use to interact with the gods in and out of Pathworking. Then, as you start independent work within the system, you can explore what you want to do.

It's always tempting to get caught up in the complexities of symbolism! Belisama is the goddess and ruler of elemental Air, which in a certain sense is entirely within the lowest sphere of Naf. The element of Air also resonates with the 8th sphere, Byth, but that doesn't make Mabon, the god of Byth, a rival god of elemental Air! Eventually you will encounter all the gods of the elements and the Tree, but that takes time and much growth. Keep going and let the path lead you.

replies.title: Re: A strong encounter in the grove of air
replies.content:
Thank you. I am glad to hear this is something that can be expected to happen from time to time. It is odd to say but in a way it was one of the loveliest experiences of my life thus far. Though I suppose I needed that, at that particular moment. I know better than to wander down the path of an astral junkie though.

I am definitely sticking to the program, and as far as I can see now, am very likely to start and finish the last grade too. What the path has done to me and to the people around me is of such immense value that I can only logically assume that doing the whole thing and keeping on doing it even afterwards will be equally, if not more, worth the time and effort.

By the way, I am tempted to ask, after immersing myself in the additional bardic lectures, might this whole thing be partly an attempt by you to actually give birth to Hesse's Glasperlenspiel?

Very much so. Have you ever noticed that nobody treats predators as meat animals?* The higher up the food chain you go, the more structured the etheric body becomes, and the more disruptive to the etheric body of the eater the etheric content of the meat becomes. That's why most human cultures make plant foods (which have very loosely structured etheric bodies) the mainstay of their diets, and supplement it with meat that comes from herbivores.

*Yes, I know some cultures eat dogs. Dogs are omnivores who feed well down the food chain, and dogs raised for meat are usually special breeds that eat a diet very high in vegetables.

Humans are apex pack predators. In fact, because of the unusually good eye-hand coordination and depth perception we inherit from our primate ancestors, and our clever trick of making hunting weapons, we're among the most ferocious and successful predators in the history of the planet. Our ancestors exterminated the cave bear, the sabertooth cat, and dozens of other fierce predator species, and we did it with stone-tipped wooden spears!

Eating human flesh is therefore extremely risky, because our etheric bodies are so highly structured. Anyone who eats "long pig" risks disrupting their own etheric body, as chunks of unprocessed etheric structure get wedged in the etheric body. That opens the way to any number of nasty intrusions from the Unseen, starting with larvae -- mindless parasitic creatures that survive by feeding on the cast-off etheric bodies of the dead -- and going down from there into the demonic realm.

replies.title: (no subject)

Interesting. This is also the case for Proto-Indo-European and several derived languages. A lot of other language groups have languages that still retain the dual too. This has me wondering: Are there language groups in which no language has a dual?

The deeper reason I ask is that singular-dual-plural seems to reflect binaries/ternaries quite well. You go from the dual binary to the plural ternary and now you're no longer specifying exactly how many options there are, suggesting that even more are possible.

If all languages initially started out with an in-built binary-expansion system, so to speak, I wonder if people naturally thought in ternaries before the Lemurian deviation and mostly lost that ability in the process.

replies.title: (no subject)

I think it's a little more complex and, shall we say, more gendered than that. Vegan males are generally feminized -- you'll find very few of them that are really robustly masculine. Carnivorous females tend toward the tomboy pattern. I doubt this is accidental; in hunter-gatherer times, men ate more meat -- they spent a lot of time out on the hunt, and had to feed themselves on their prey before bringing as much as they could back to camp. Women ate more plant foods -- they ate some of what they gathered to keep going before bringing the rest back to camp to share. Since hunters need to be comfortable with being cut off from the collective consciousness of their tribe for anything up to weeks at a time, while gatherers benefit from being part of a collective mind and working together. Thus human psychobiology evolved toward a model where meat became a trigger for a more solitary and independent consciousness, while its absence became a trigger for a more group-dependent consciousness.

replies.content:
1) It's purely a matter of having a mental sheath and thus being in the transitional state between material and transmaterial incarnation. Souls incarnate as squirrels as part of working out their destiny in animal incarnation, and will go on to become many other kinds of animal; humans, whales, and the few other animal forms that have mental sheaths are transitioning out of material incarnation and going through a lot of change very fast, after which they don't have material bodies again.

2) It's the other way around. A human soul that disperses its mental sheath by relentlessly turning away from the realm of meaning and value is then born in an animal form, since it doesn't have a mental sheath to bring it back into human incarnation.

3) A raw divine spark surrounded by a body of sorts, composed of the waste products of earlier universes. The phrase "Eaters of Dung" sometimes used for them is by no means wholly inaccurate.

4) That's correct. We shed our etheric and astral bodies at intervals thereafter but the mental body is the first of our permanent bodies, so there's no further loss of memories and continuity of consciousness.

5) Not quite. The fae have material bodies but those are composed mostly of air and energy, rather than mostly of solids and liquids as ours are.

6) They have to shed their existing bodies. For them, that's either a voluntary act or something that's forced on them by a sorcerer; either way, it's unnatural and traumatic for them.

7) If you banish a lot, be sure to invoke a lot. The Sphere of Protection contains both invoking and banishing components, so can be done several times a day. Have you considered protective natural magic, though? That would be my first suggestion.

To my mind, it depends on just how toxic the effects of mass media (and especially social media) turn out to be. I've begun developing a concept I call "cognitive collapse" -- a phenomenon in which apparently intelligent people lose the ability to relate abstractions to meanings, and retreat into the repetition of verbal formulae. Some of the things I witnessed during the Covid business inspired that concept, but it's quite common these days in a range of contexts.

I think it's the equivalent of model collapse in LLMs ("AIs"). In case you aren't familiar with this, model collapse happens when LLMs are trained on the output of other LLMs, and their ability to produce output with any relation to the real world implodes. In the same way, I suspect that the feedback loops of social media, which feed each person whatever infotainment they prefer, may cause "model collapse" in the human mind and result in the phenomenon I've observed. If that happens on the large scale, we could get substantial herds of herbivores very quickly...

replies.content:
Dion Fortune talks about this in one of her essays, and also one of the Dr. Taverner stories. She says it happens at rare intervals, usually when the mother is alcoholic and happens to get very drunk near a place where fae manifest. The children are socially clueless, casually vicious, and quickly become addicted to drink and drugs, and they leave a trail of carnage around them because they have no idea how to be human. They also age fast and die young, because their subtle bodies are very poorly suited for a human body.

If the person you know has these characteristics, then I suppose it's possible. If not, well, I've met people who claim to have been magical dragons in past incarnations, and I've also met people who insist that they're married (in some hard to define spiritual sense) to the fictional character Severus Snape. Confabulating romantic past incarnations as a way to make up for a dreary life this time around has been a common sport since Theosophical times.

The late Francis King, a fine occultist and writer, used to say that he had personally met no fewer than 70 people who claimed to be the one and only reincarnation of Aleister Crowley. He claimed that he believed them all, since each of them demonstrated about 1/70th of Crowley's intelligence and wit! His point, of course, is that if somebody claims to have been someone really remarkable in a previous life, they had damn well better be at least as remarkable in this one.

comments.title: Western Occult and Thinking
comments.content:
Hello John Michael,

Does the Western Occult tradition have tools to help improve everyday mental skills?

I mean everyone has their strengths and weaknesses. Some people can play a half-hour piano sonata from memory. Other good musicians can't get through "Happy Birthday" without a mistake.

Some people can multiply any pair of two-digit numbers in their head, others struggle with, say, figuring out if it is 2 am in NY, what time is it in CA?

What if someone wanted to sit and think some great thoughts not thunk before and never been able to do it?

Does the Western Occult tradition have tools for improving mental skills in a desired direction?

I welcome comments from the commentariat, too.

replies.title: Re: Western Occult and Thinking
replies.content:
We had them. Keep in mind that the Western occult traditions have passed through two harrowing periods when persecution and general neglect caused much of the old teachings to be lost. The first was after the fall of Rome, when established Christian churches tried to stamp out most of what survived of the old magic; the second was after the end of the Renaissance, when established churches made a devil's bargain with the rising movement of scientific materialism to do the same thing. What we have now are fragments of what was once a far more complete whole.

These days? There are people, a fair number of them, reviving the old Art of Memory -- Frances Yates' volume The Art of Memory, though it's a history of the art rather than a textbook, is a good introduction. A few people are beginning to work in other directions. If you want to join them, get a good solid foundation in occult philosophy and practice first.

replies.title: Re: Western Occult and Thinking
replies.content: New Thought had mental magic, the most famous being William Walker Atkinson's series he wrote under the pen name Theron Q. Dumont.  See "The Master Mind" and "The Power of Concentration" for starters (in that order).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Every few decades somebody comes up with a version of magic that's been watered down so it fits the prejudices of our rationalist and materialist society. Chaos magic is one of those. I think of it the way I think of light beer -- a pale and tasteless substitute for the rich strong brew I prefer. As for sigils, the version of those used in chaos magic is a simple but moderately effective tool, roughly on a par with affirmations. I prefer affirmations because I naturally think in spoken language; if I had a naturally strong visual imagination I'd probably use sigils.

3) I've heard of some but I don't know any of them personally. Most of my contacts with the tradition has been with ex-chaos magicians who got tired of getting no results from their workings but sticky fingers, and wanted to learn something more effective; that may well bias my opinion of the tradition.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Last week there was a brief discussion about occult sleep aids. This answer of mine is not occult, though it is lesser known outside certain circles. If JMG will permit it to be put through I wanted to suggest the use of red- or amber-colored incandescent light bulbs after sunset. This has helped my own sleep a lot.

replies.title: medieval sleep patterns
replies.content:
Before getting to sleep aids, it's a good idea to get out of the closed thinking behind the idea that a single continuous sleep of 8 hours is normal and anything other than that is abnormal.

In medieval times they had first and second sleep / segmented sleep with a period of wakefulness that could include going visiting!

Modern society seems like a structure of polished turds, and the view of sleep is a big one - searching on medieval sleep patterns and habits makes for some fascinating reading - of course, there is now a proper scientific term for what those ignorant peasants [/sarc] were up to: polyphasic sleep

replies.title: Re: medieval sleep patterns
replies.content: Yes, I'm familiar with this and have tried it for myself for a few short times. I found it very nice. It's lovely to have a period of wakefulness when there aren)t so many thoughts and feelings flying around (that's one thing I love about time spent in Spain - when siesta time comes around for the Madrileños, the sensitive traveller gets a little midday relative solitude). I do a fair amount of touring in my musical career which often forces one to break up their sleep into a bit at the hotel, a bit more on the train, etc etc. There's a learning curve for that but once one gets used to it, it's pretty easy to function at peak in the body and the mind. I agree that conventional ideas on sleep are certainly not the only game in town. But, for those who for one reason or another want to make deep, continuous sleep through the night work for them, the light bulb thing seems to be one of a few methods that effectively assist with that MO.

replies.title: Re: medieval sleep patterns
replies.content:
Fair to say - most people's lives in this world are tied to the system. When I discovered the idea of first and second sleep it was something solid to consider and neatly removed concern/worry about the heavily pushed 8 hr block and goes towards sidestepping the marketing of pill pushers who profit from the idea.

Coming to the idea that waking up in the night is okay removes a barrier to sleep; it does not of course solve insomnia.

We've tried low blue computer monitors but where you say:
"I wanted to suggest the use of red- or amber-colored incandescent light bulbs after sunset."

How exactly are you using them? We use salt lamps but turn them off to sleep.

replies.title: Re: medieval sleep patterns
replies.content:
Once the sun's gone down, I use them as a light source in my room by which to read and work. I use red myself, which is a little dim and takes some getting used to. Amber may be a more practical choice. They seem to be helpful in that they don't disturb the circadian rhythm as much as LEDs or brighter bulbs, which means that when bedtime comes the body is more ready for it, and not flooded with the stress induced by LED lights, ehuch in turn means less cortisol in the system and less chance of it awaking one in the night. At least this is my experience, i won't claim to be an expert or even especially informed about what I'm doing, but I have noticed a difference.

It sounds as if you might basically be doing the same thing with your salt lamps.

replies.title: Re: medieval sleep patterns
replies.content:
When shopping for light bulbs, look at the "color temperature." Lower temperatures are more yellow and red, while higher temperatures tend toward stark white or blue. Incandescent light bulbs are the closest thing to sunshine, with a color temperature between 2700K and 3000K.

When I buy LED light bulbs, I look for a color temperature around 2500 or 2700. That gives the room a calming, warm feeling, and won't shock you awake when you turn them on after dark.

replies.title: Re:sleep patterns
replies.content:
Maybe a few not so magical datapoints relating to "good sleep":
- one radio station over here in Germany had a sleep-scientist (Schlaf-Forscher) this year in a show who said that sound sleep equalling 8-10 hours uninterruptes sleep is a misconception. People accourding to his (and more general) studies need 45 minutes intervals (not sure how many in a row) and as long as one gets those there is no problem with waking up (several times) during the night.
- A client of mine had heard that one should sleep multiples of 1.5 hours to wake up fit. When he remembered to do so, he really felt fitter.
- there´s also often a personal pattern in how much sleep one needs, a friend of mine could manage very well on 4 hours when he was a youngster and now (being in his 40ies with a kid) manages well on 5-6 hours. I let you guess what kind of alfabetical soup might to be had if diagnosis would start.
- you are probably aware of this: in the praxis where I work as an OT we often let parents of children who are diagnosed with autism and have problems sleeping experiment with a weighted blanket, often with excellent results.
- once upon a time I had an internship where I felt quite alone in the world - to ameliorate that lonely feeling I went to bed snuggling up to a heavy coat, which helped me. YMMV, still - if I may say so: maybe you could tease out what wakes you up and then find a substitue? I´m thinking of e.g. Commander Vimes from Terry Pratchets books who is a truebred city-kid and had to stay over a holiday in the country and couldn´t sleep because the sound environment was totally different.
best wishes, Emily07

comments.title: Intuition
comments.content:
I wonder how to learn to tell intuition from fantasy, for lack of better terms.

In everyday situations that involve a certain degree of physical proximity with the opposite gender, I sometimes get image flashes.

Those can involve sexual situations not really to my taste, with a distinctly different quality than fantasies I would harbor about people I find attractive.
Sometimes, sudden sparks of interest for someone I didn't find attractive before (like a sudden urge saying "we have to kiss" - where did that come from?)

At other times, sudden ideas about the worst things a certain type of person would be capable of doing.

I wonder if the first kind is some way of perceiving other people's thoughts/feelings/wishes, or my own projection. It seems to need the physical closeness.

For the second kind, I attributed it to family trauma (labor camp) and the need to be able to tell quickly how bad it can get with the person in front of you.

Do you know of any way to learn to interpret such things?

Thank you as always and have a good trip!

replies.title: Re: Intuition
replies.content: That's always a challenge. With me, genuine intuitions are formless -- there are no words or images associated with them, just a sudden knowing -- and I learned to trust them only via lots of experience. Your own intuitive gifts may be different, of course, and I don't know of a reliable way to sort things out other than lots of practice. Maybe some of my readers may be able to suggest something else.

I then walked the shore & tried to relax my thinking. I had the thought of something like "Just love us. Don't treat us like a 'natural resource' to exploit. comments.title: SOP Variations
comments.content:
More than two years ago, I started practicing the SOP (dreamwidth version) on a daily version. During the last months I experimented with a few variations. Behind this are two main reasons: First of all, my schedule only allows for a regular practice in the late evenings. Due to external constraints, there are times when I am extremely tired in the evening and so I tried to create an occult "minimum practice" to help weathering these phases which usually last a working week of 5 or 6 days. And secondly, due to allergies and the occasional cold there are not so infrequent phases when my nose is so congested that it is a pain for me to speak, especially in the evenings.

Here's what I've tried:
1) Standard SOP but with the invocation and banishing words spoken in my mind. - Franky, it feels pretty powerless.
2) "Compacted" SOP with the visualization taking place during speaking the invocation and banishing (I don't know if everybody does this already, but I usually speak first and then concentrate on the visualization). This seems to give the SOP a very strong boost and a kind of sharpness, makes it even shorter at the same time - but I found it to be quite exhausting.
3) "Compacted" with the vocal parts almost entirely left out except for the vibrations. This feels as powerful as the standard SOP that I am used to, maybe even more. One aspect is especially interesting - I don't speak the the phrases of the invocation and banishing in my mind but intended to go directly to the visualization. But then an "astral analog" to the invocation entered my thoughts, something that I can only describe as an imagery containing the intention of the words that I usually speak. Since the SOP works mainly on the astral plane, this may be a good thing, I guess.

All in all - I didn't have good results with 1), had very strong and interesting results with 2) with some drawbacks, though, and I like 3) very much especially because it increases the meditative aspect of the SOP. During straining times I now try to stick with SOP v.3 as serving as both ritual and meditation accompanied by daily divination and quick journaling which takes about 12-15 minutes. During more relaxed times I do the full "occult trinity" which easily takes 30 minutes or more. Variation and alternation between the different versions of the SOP seems to generally be a good thing, since the standard version of the SOP gets an additional boost when switching back from "v.3" and vice versa. And lastly, I got the feeling that is not a good idea to begin the practice with a non-vocal variation or do it for an extended period of time. Speaking the words over and over again for years seems to do and build up things and I could imagine that lacking or neglecting this practice may cause problems in the long run.

So, these are a few data points to share. Maybe an old hat for many? Anyway, if there's anything to comment, advice or criticize I'd be grateful. And if anybody else has experimented along these lines and is willing to share, I'd be grateful too!

Have a good day!

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: SOP Variations
replies.content: Interesting -- thanks for this. I've always had time to do the full form, so it's useful to know about these compacted forms.

replies.title: Re: SOP Variations
replies.content: Hello, I have some experience with approach 1 while traveling or similar.  I find it helpful to imagine (but not pretend) that the inner voice is the same substance as all of the imagery.  This creates a sort of unified field with the internally spoken invocations and substance of the sphere.  With practice this turns from imagination to a felt experience.  Luke Z
